segments.text: Lesson 7
To understand Japanese negatives we need one of the most important ideas in Japanese grammar: the adjective ない.

ない means:
“does not exist”.
“is not”.

For inanimate things the verb for “exist” is ある.
So:
“ぺん が ある”

This means:
“There is a pen.”

But if we want to say there is no pen we say:
“ぺん が ない”

This means:
“There is no pen.”
In Japanese doing something uses a verb. But not doing something is treated as a state of non-action so it uses ない which works like an い-adjective.

That is why negatives in Japanese are built with ない.
Now if we want to say “This is a pen” we say:
“これ は ぺん だ”

This means:
“As for this it is a pen.”

But if we want to say “This is not a pen” we say:
“これ は ぺん では ない”

This means:
“This is not a pen.”
Here で is the て-form of だ. So the structure is like saying:
“As for this as for being a pen it is not.”

Now let’s look at negative verbs.
To make a verb negative we attach ない to the あ-stem.
Japanese verbs end in a う-row kana. To make the あ-stem we move the final kana from the う-row to the あ-row.

For example:
“きく”: “きか”
“はなす”: “はなさ”
“もつ”: “もた”

Then we add ない:
“きく”: “きかない”: “does not hear”
“はなす”: “はなさない”: “does not speak”
“もつ”: “もたない”: “does not hold”

There is one small exception.
When a verb ends in う the あ-stem becomes わ not あ.

So:
“かう”: “かわない”
Not:
“かあない”

This makes pronunciation easier.
With いちだん verbs the rule is even simpler. We remove る and add ない.

For example:
“たべる”: “たべない”

This means:
“does not eat”

So the basic rule is:
“ごだん verbs”: change the final う-row kana to the あ-row and add ない
“いちだん verbs”: remove る and add ない

Now let’s look at adjectives.
To change an い-adjective we usually turn the final い into く.

For example:
“あかい”: “あかく”
“かわいい”: “かわいく”
“こわい”: “こわく”

To make the negative we add ない:
“あかい”: “あかくない”: “is not red”
“かわいい”: “かわいくない”: “is not cute”
“こわい”: “こわくない”: “is not scary”
To make an い-adjective past tense we remove い and add かった.

For example:
“こわい”: “こわかった”

This means:
“was scary”
Because ない is also an い-adjective its past form is:
“なかった”

So if we want to say “was not scary” we say:
“こわくなかった”

Now let’s apply this to verbs.
“さくら が はしる”

This means:
“Sakura runs” or “Sakura will run.”

To say “Sakura does not run” we say:
“さくら が はしらない”
はしる is a ごだん verb so る changes to ら and then we add ない.

To say “Sakura ran” we say:
“さくら が はしった”

To say “Sakura did not run” we say:
“さくら が はしらなかった”

The logic is simple:
“はしらない”: does not run
“はしらなかった”: did not run

We put ない into the past by changing it to なかった.
Now let’s use the more natural continuous form.

Instead of:
“さくら が はしる”
we often say:
“さくら が はしっている”

This means:
“Sakura is running.”

To put this into the past we change いる into いた:
“さくら が はしっていた”

This means:
“Sakura was running.”

To make it negative past we change いる into いなかった:
“さくら が はしっていなかった”

This means:
“Sakura was not running.”

いる is an いちだん verb so it follows the regular pattern:
“いる”: “いた”
“いる”: “いない”
“いる”: “いなかった”

Japanese works like building blocks. Once you know the basic parts you can combine them to make many forms.
There are only a couple of important exceptions here.

The first is ます.
ます is a helper verb that makes verbs formal.

For example:
“はなす”: “はなします”
“きく”: “ききます”
Normally we might expect the negative of ます to be something like “まさない” but it is not.

The negative of ます is:
“ません”

So:
“はなします”: “はなしません”
“ききます”: “ききません”

This is because formal Japanese keeps an older negative form: せん.
The second exception is the adjective いい meaning “is good”.

いい has an older form:
“よい”

When we transform いい it goes back to よい.
So the past form is not:
“いかった”

It is:
“よかった”

This means:
“was good”

And the negative is not:
“いくない”

It is:
“よくない”

This means:
“is not good”
So the main patterns are:
“ある”: “ない”: exists / does not exist
“ぺん が ない”: there is no pen
“これ は ぺん では ない”: this is not a pen
“きく”: “きかない”: does not hear
“はなす”: “はなさない”: does not speak
“たべる”: “たべない”: does not eat
“あかい”: “あかくない”: is not red
“こわい”: “こわかった”: was scary
“こわい”: “こわくなかった”: was not scary
“はしる”: “はしらない”: does not run
“はしる”: “はしらなかった”: did not run
“はしっている”: “はしっていた”: was running
“はしっている”: “はしっていなかった”: was not running
“ます”: “ません”: formal negative
“いい”: “よかった”: was good
“いい”: “よくない”: is not good
